coredata.dc:title: Core-shell spherical cerium oxide, N-doped carbon dots and copper oxide nanocomposites with oxidase-like activity for colorimetric detection of hexavalent chromiums
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, Mingzhu
Guo, Peiqing
Lei, Xuefang
Li, Yujin
Li, Shaohui
Meng, Ran
Zhou, Xibin
coredata.dc:description: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) is a particular toxic chemical not only hazardous to human health but also causes environmental pollution. It can diffuse in the environment and has a tendency to accumulate in living organisms, so chromium detection is important for environmental protection. In this work, a colorimetric method for the determination of Cr(VI) in water based on the solvothermal synthesis of cerium oxide doped carbon quantum dot composite copper oxides (CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs) with oxidase-like activity. The particles were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR). The 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was used as a chromogenic substrate, and CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs exhibited significant oxidase-like activity in the presence of Cr(VI). The method was able to successfully perform colorimetric detection of Cr(VI). The method is independent of potential interfering ions and has excellent selectivity for the detection of Cr(VI). Tap water testing verified the feasibility of the method for the analysis of real samples. Hence, we anticipate that the method can successfully create a unique portable high-throughput visual sensing platform for Cr(VI) detection, opening up a new approach for testing real water samples in the field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxidase-like
Cr(VI)
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chromium (VI) as an initiator of oxidase-like activity of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs
Factors affecting the enzyme-mimic activity of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs
Ultrasensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI)
Analysis application in Yellow River and tap water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images; (B) SAED pattern; and (C) EDS mapping of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs.
XRD patterns of (a) CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs; (b) CuO and (c) CeO2/N-CQDs.
(A) Survey and high-resolution; (B) C 1 s; (C) N 1 s; (C) O 1 s; (E) Ce 3d; (F) Cu 2p XPS spectra of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs.
The UV–vis spectra of different systems (A), steady-state kinetic analyses using the Michaelis–Menten model (B) and Lineweaver–Burk model (C) for CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs.

The effect of experimental conditions on the sensing performance (A) the pH (B) the concentration of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs (C) the concentration of TMB (D) the reaction time between Cr(VI) + TMB+ CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs system.
(A) Absorption spectra at different concentrations of Cr(VI); (B) The linear relationship between absorption signal and Cr(VI) concentration (Inset is below 0.5 μM Cr (VI) the points clusters).
Selectivity of colorimetric sensing for different metal cations, Cr(III) and Cr(VI). (10-fold concentration of other metal ions and Cr(Ⅲ) relative to Cr(VI)).
CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs stimulate higher oxidase-like activity in the presence of Cr(VI).
Comparison between the LOD and linear range of the method in this work and other reported methods applied for Cr(VI) quantification via enzyme-mimic activities.
Determination results of Cr(VI) in tap water.
Determination results of Cr(VI) in Yellow River.

head.ce:title: Core-shell spherical cerium oxide, N-doped carbon dots and copper oxide nanocomposites with oxidase-like activity for colorimetric detection of hexavalent chromiums

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Resource Environment and Sustainable Development of Oasis, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) is a particular toxic chemical not only hazardous to human health but also causes environmental pollution. It can diffuse in the environment and has a tendency to accumulate in living organisms, so chromium detection is important for environmental protection. In this work, a colorimetric method for the determination of Cr(VI) in water based on the solvothermal synthesis of cerium oxide doped carbon quantum dot composite copper oxides (CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs) with oxidase-like activity. The particles were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR). The 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was used as a chromogenic substrate, and CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs exhibited significant oxidase-like activity in the presence of Cr(VI). The method was able to successfully perform colorimetric detection of Cr(VI). The method is independent of potential interfering ions and has excellent selectivity for the detection of Cr(VI). Tap water testing verified the feasibility of the method for the analysis of real samples. Hence, we anticipate that the method can successfully create a unique portable high-throughput visual sensing platform for Cr(VI) detection, opening up a new approach for testing real water samples in the field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mobile hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) is a highly toxic carcinogen mainly present in wastewater from metal plating, leather tanning, textile printing, and dyeing industries [1,2]. Chromium exists in two main valence states: Cr(III) and Cr(VI), which have different chemical properties and toxicological characteristics [3,4]. Cr(III) is essential for lipid and sugar metabolism and is a trace element necessary for human and animal health. Whereas Cr(VI) is toxic, accumulation in the body can damage the respiratory and digestive systems and is highly carcinogenic [5]. In the aqueous environment, Cr(VI) is usually present in the form of anions such as chromate (CrO4 2-), dichromate (Cr2O7 2-) and hydrochromate (HCrO4 -). Given its high mobility and toxicity, which are hazardous to water quality, the World Health Organization (WHO) stipulates that the level of total chromium in drinking water should not exceed 50 μg/L [6]. There have been many methods of detecting Cr(VI). These include ion chromatography (IC), atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and diphenyl carbon spectrophotometry [7–10]. Among them, IC, AAS and HPLC have high selectivity and sensitivity, but require complicated pre-treatment steps and relatively expensive instruments; whereas, although less expensive, diphenyl carbon spectrophotometry suffers from unstable color rendering and many interferences, which limitations cause inconvenience for on-site detection. Therefore, it is of great practical significance and application value to develop a rapid and economical portable on-site detection method.

Different types of methods to facilitate the detection of Cr(VI) in the hydrosphere are under development, such as fluorescent probes, electrochemical and colorimetric methods [11–13]. Included among these, fluorescent probe methods are greatly influenced by environmental conditions [14,15]. For example, environmental factors such as light intensity and humidity may affect the performance and stability of the fluorescent probe. In addition, in certain sample matrices, the fluorescence of the fluorescent probe may be affected by matrix effects such as light absorption, fluorescence burst or fluorescence enhancement. When electrochemical methods are used to detect actual water samples, the surfaces of the electrodes used are susceptible to contamination by impurities, metal ions, or organic matter in the sample [16,17]. This contamination may cause the electrodes to become inactive, directly interfering with the measurement process or reducing the accuracy of the results. Compared with fluorescent probe and electrochemical methods, colorimetric methods have the advantages of easy operation, low cost, wide range of application, high reliability, and the results can be read directly by the naked eye or UV–visible spectrophotometer without complicated procedures and cumbersome operations [18]. In recent years, nanozyme-based sensors have become important colorimetric measurement tools due to their inherent advantages of good stability and ease of preparation, and the interest of many researchers in their research and application has been increasing[19]. Sehrish and colleagues proposed a method for colorimetric detection of Cr(VI) based on metallic palladium as an oxidase mimic[20]. Analogously, gold nanoparticles and silver nanoparticles are also frequently used to construct chromium chemosensors for colorimetric detection [21,22]. However, precious metal nanoparticles are costly and susceptible to aggregation, precipitation, or morphological changes due to environmental factors (pH, temperature, and concentration of chemicals in solution), leading to a decrease in the stability of the material, which affects its use in environmental waters for quantitative identification of environmental targets [23]. Therefore, inexpensive metal oxide nanomaterials are beginning to be of interest to researchers. Interestingly, both cerium oxide and copper oxide have been shown to possess oxidase-like activities that activate dissolved oxygen to form superoxide anion, which oxidizes the chromogenic substrate [24,25].

Cerium oxide (CeO2) has been used in various fields widely such as catalysis, detection, and adsorption given high oxide concentration, structural defects, and excellent physicochemical properties, which exhibit good mimetic enzyme activity [26–28]. The double oxidation states of Ce(III) and Ce(IV) significantly influence their chemical composition, resulting in cerium oxide (CeO2) nanomaterials with multiple enzyme activities [29–31]. CeO2 nanozyme offers several advantages over noble metal-based nanozyme, including low cost, high abundance, and environmental friendliness. Nevertheless, pure CeO2 has little nanozymatic activity and requires an extended period for it to communicate with the substrate, necessitating surface modification [32–34]. Various approaches have been used previously to enhance enzyme-like activity based on CeO2 structures, such as composite nanomaterials formed by hybridization with metal oxides (MnO2, Fe2O3), carbon materials (graphene) and biopolymers [35–38]. However, since many metal oxide particles do not carry a surface charge at the isoelectric point, they tend to reaggregate, resulting in a loss of specific surface area, which may lead to a decrease in enzyme activity [39,40]. To prevent the formation of such highly aggregated systems, we introduced carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) to encapsulate the CeO2 prior to hybridization with CuO to preserve their catalytic surface.

In this work, cerium oxide doped carbon quantum dot composite copper oxides (CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs) were synthesized by a simple solvothermal method. The in situ growth of copper oxide was assisted by a solvothermal reaction after N-doped carbon dots were wrapped around the surface of cerium oxide in the absence of high reaction temperatures and complex operations. Furthermore, to the best of our knowledge, although CeO2 and CuO have been used as enzyme mimics for the detection of Cr(VI), respectively, this is the first report that the incorporation of N-CQDs improves the aggregation of CeO2 and CuO, resulting in a much higher oxidase activity of the synthesized CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs. In addition, since Cr(VI) stimulates the oxidase-like activity of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs, which uses dissolved oxygen as an oxidant, it is able to rapidly oxidize a colorless TMB solution into a dark blue oxidation product (TMBox), the color of which increases proportionally to the concentration of Cr(VI). Therefore, CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs can be used for the sensitive detection of Cr(VI) by colorimetric methods. However, since the activity of oxidizing enzymes is related to dissolved oxygen, the application in actual water samples can meet most of the needs, but it is not applicable to special water samples, such as water samples with too low dissolved oxygen. This method avoids the use of unstable and environmentally unfriendly H2O2 as an oxidizing agent. Importantly, the proposed colorimetric method is highly sensitive and selective for Cr(VI), which provides a promising application for the in situ determination of Cr(VI) in real water samples.

All reagents are commercially available and used according to purchase instructions. The Aladdin Chemical Company provided copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2⋅3 H2O, 99%), cerium nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3⋅6 H2O, 99.5%), citric acid (CA, 99.5%), urea (≥99.5%), and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, ≥99%). The National Pharmaceutical Chemical Reagent Company was the source of ethylene glycol (EG, ≥99.5%) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, M.W. 40000). The analysis-grade reagents and metal ion solution were available for use without further purification. The experiment utilized deionized water as the primary source.

The Mapada UV spectrophotometer (UV-1800, CHN) was used to collect UV visible absorption spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEITECNAI20, USA) was used to acquire images of the nanocomposites in order to analyze their morphological and structural properties. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, USA) to analyze the surface composition and chemical state of the products. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, FTS3000, USA) was performed to determine the molecular structure and chemical bonding of the materials. X-ray diffraction (XRD, SHIMADZU, JPN) was used to analyze the crystal characteristics. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, BRUKER EMXPLUS, DE) was used to detect free radicals in the samples. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Agilent 7700, USA) was used to verify the acceptability and accuracy of the experimental system. Hydrothermal reactions in Jinghong high temperature furnace (GHG-9030A, CHN). pH meter (Lei Ci PHS-3 C, CHN) was used to adjust the pH of the buffer.

Referring to the previous studies on the synthesis of N-CQDs [41]. 50 mL of deionized water was used to dissolve 2.1 g of citric acid and 1.8 g of urea, which were then stirred for 3 h at room temperature. After being moved into a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with Teflon, the combined solution was continuously heated in an oven at 160°C for 8 h. The fluid was dialyzed for two days in a 3500 Da dialysis bag after cooling to room temperature and replaced with deionized water every 12 h.

CeO2/N-CQDs NPs were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction. Typically, Ce(NO3)3⋅6 H2O (0.3333 g), PVP (0.1333 g), and N-CQDs prepared as described above were distributed in EG (10 mL) solution (10 mL), deionized water (350 μL) was added, and the mixture was rapidly agitated for 1 h at the ambient temperature. Subsequently, it was moved to a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with PTFE and prepared continuously for 8 h at 160°C in an oven. Allow the mixed solution to cool to room temperature, centrifuged for 8 min at 12000 rpm, and then collect by rinsing with ethanol and deionized water in turn. Next, equal amounts of CeO2/N-CQDs NPs and Cu(NO3)2⋅3 H2O were mixed for 1 h after dissolving in 15 mL of deionized water. The aforementioned solution was then mixed with diluted ammonia and heated in an oil bath at 40 °C for 4 h of continuous reaction. Finally, the obtained suspension was centrifuged for 10 min at 13000 r/min and collected.

The oxidase-like catalytic activities of CuO, CeO2, CuO/CeO2, and CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs were investigated by catalyzing the oxidation reaction of TMB comparatively. Briefly, CuO, CeO2, CeO2/CuO, or CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs (2.50 mg·mL−1) were added to citrate-sodium citrate buffer (1.0 M, pH 3.2) containing 0.35 mM TMB. Subsequently, Cr(VI) (1.0 μM) was added and the spectrophotometer was used to measure the values of absorbance at 652 nm. Moreover, the catalytic reaction conditions of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs were tuned for various pH, TMB concentrations, and reaction durations.

A constant concentration of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs and Cr(VI) were combined with varying quantities of TMB as the substrate to study the steady-state mechanism of the catalytic oxidation reaction of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs. Under ideal experimental circumstances, kinetic evaluations of the catalytic response were carried out by tracking the absorbance value at 652 nm. The Michaelis-Menten equation was utilized to obtain the kinetic parameters.

The colorimetric determination of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs with and without Cr(VI) in solution. In general, the addition of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs (2.50 mg·mL−1) and a certain concentration of Cr(VI) to the TMB colorimetric system resulted in a significant color change, which did not occur in the absence of Cr(VI). Specifically, 1.83 mL pH 3.2 citric acid-sodium citrate buffer was supplemented with 50 μL CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs, 20 μL of Cr(VI) solutions at varying concentrations, and 100 μL of TMB (7 mM) solution, in that order. Cr(VI) was measured using a UV spectrophotometer set to 652 nm. In addition, the blue reaction products were monitored by a quartz 96-well enzyme labeling plate. Furthermore, the reaction phenomena of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs with Cr(VI) and other ions were investigated under the same experimental conditions comparatively.

Samples of river water were taken from the Yellow River in Gansu Province Lanzhou City. Samples of tap water were collected at Northwest Normal University new campus. Filtration of water samples using 0.22 μm microporous membranes. Subsequently, 1.83 mL of aqueous sample were mixed with CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs (50 μL) and TMB (100 μL), and incubated under optimal conditions. In the recovery test, various concentrations of Cr(VI) solutions were added to water samples containing a mixture of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs and TMB for incubation under optimal conditions. The results were analyzed by standard spiking method and the corresponding spiking recoveries were calculated.

Scheme 1 describes the possible process for the synthesis of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs systematically. Ce3+ ions were firstly encapsulated by a large number of N-CQDs nanoparticles to form precursor nanomaterials (CeO2/N-CQDs NPs), and then with the aid of an oil bath in which CuO was attached to the surface of CeO2/N-CQDs NPs to form spherical CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs successfully. The prepared CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs were characterized by TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, and EPR. TEM was used to characterize the shape and dimension of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs. As shown in Fig. S1, TEM images showed that N-CQDs were uniformly distributed nanoparticles with an average diameter of about 2.12 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. S1A), and the CeO2/N-CQDs NPs exhibited a flower-like morphology with a diameter of about 100 nm (Fig. S1B). Interestingly, after the successful growth of CuO on the CeO2/N-CQDs NPs surface, CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs have an obvious core-shell structure, which had a spherical shape with a diameter of roughly 200 nm (Fig. S1C and 1A). In addition, SAED (selected area electron diffractogram) revealed the presence of localized crystallization of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs ( Fig. 1B). Furthermore, the obtained CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs were also analyzed by elemental mapping (Fig. 1 C). It is evident that the components C, N, O, Ce, and Cu are distributed inside the CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs framework, indicating that the CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs were prepared successfully.

XRD was used to analyze the crystal structures of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs, CuO, and CeO2/N-CQDs. Fig. 2 evident that the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28.2°, 33.1°, 47.5°, and 56.3° belong to the (002) facet of N-CQDs and the (200), (220), and (311) facets of CeO2, respectively. The results indicate that CeO2/N-CQDs NPs have been synthesized successfully. Additionally, the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 32.5°, 35.4°, 38.7°, 48.7°, 56.7°and 61.5° belong to the (110), (002), (111), (20−2), (202), and (11−3) facets of CuO, respectively. The results showed that CuO was complexed with CeO2/N-CQDs NPs successfully. The comparison curves demonstrate that the basic crystal structure of the CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs was unaffected by the CuO covering. Surface group alterations were examined using FT-IR spectroscopy. The unique peaks at 1041 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1, as displayed in Fig. S2, are corresponding to C-N and -CONH- on N-CQDs, respectively. In addition, the band between 1200 and 1600 cm−1 belongs to the Ce-O oscillations, which is attributed to the interference caused by bound CeO2 probably[42]. To sum up, we can declare firmly that N-CQDs were loaded successfully.

To further examine the chemical states of C, N, O, Ce, and Cu in CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs, XPS measurements were employed ( Fig. 3). The elements C, N, O, Ce, and Cu are confirmed to be contained in CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs by the survey and high-resolution XPS spectra, as more clearly shown in Fig. 3B-F. Among them, the C 1 s spectrum can be condensed into three primary peaks, which correspond to the C-C bond, C-O bond, and CO, with respective chemical energies of 284.6, 285.8, and 288.5 eV (Fig. 3B). The spectrum of N 1 s is divided into three distinct peaks, indicating the presence of pyridine N (399.3 eV), pyrrole N (400.2 eV), and graphite N (401.1 eV) in the N-CQDs structure (Fig. 3 C). The O 1 s spectral peaks at 530.9 and 532.2 eV are associated with the absorption state O and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 3D). Furthermore, it is necessary to allocate the four sets of spin-orbit couplings of the Ce 3d spectra to the 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 orbitals, denoted as u and v, respectively (Fig. 3E). Included among these, v, v1, v2, u, u1, and u2 products of Ce(Ⅳ), while v0 and u1 products of Ce(Ⅲ) probably. These data indicate that Ce(Ⅳ) and Ce(Ⅲ) coexist in CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs. The binding energies of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 are corresponding to the two primary peaks, which are located at 932.6 and 952.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 3 F). Other satellite peaks in the spectra suggest that Cu is mostly present in the samples as Cu2+. It is commonly recognized that the unique double valence-redox cycle (Ce(IV)/Ce(III)) and oxygen vacancies are intimately related to the oxidation catalytic activity of Ce-based nanomaterials[43]. The redox cycle of coexistence of Ce(IV) and Ce(III) sites in Ce-based nanomaterials is accelerated by the strong oxidizing property of Cr(VI), which is more likely to eliminate the electrons in the substrate and encourage electron exchange. CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs may be used in Cr(VI) colorimetric detection since the addition of N-CQDs safeguards the active sites and significantly increases their catalytic oxidation activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromium (VI) as an initiator of oxidase-like activity of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The oxidase-like catalytic activity was evaluated by catalyzing the TMB chromogenic reaction in the presence of O2. Firstly, the oxidase-like activity of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs was evaluated. The CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs in solution did not show a significant response at 652 nm ( Fig. 4A). In the CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs + TMB system, there was a noticeable absorption peak at 652 nm that was linked to the creation of TMBox. This suggests that CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs have inherent oxidase-like activity. It is interesting to note that the typical absorption peaks of TMBox produced by the addition of Cr(VI) to the oxidation of TMB catalyzed by CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs are substantially stronger than those of the system without Cr(VI). The aforementioned data show that the addition of Cr(VI) increased CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs oxidase-like activity. Cr(VI) was also easily reduced to Cr(III) on CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs, replacing the typical Fenton effect caused by H2O2 [44]. Meanwhile, the oxidase-like catalytic properties of N-CQDs, CeO2, CuO, and CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs were comparatively investigated by catalyzing the TMB chromogenic reaction (Fig. S3). N-CQDs, CeO2, CuO, and Cr(VI) exhibit very little catalytic activity for the oxidation of TMB. However, the oxidase-like activity of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs was much higher, nearly 2–3 times higher than that of pure CeO2/CuO catalytic activity, and the reaction solution was much darker. In addition, the enzyme-like catalytic mechanism of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs was investigated and used to better explain TMB oxidation. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation is generally the main characteristic of the catalytic activity of oxidative enzymes. TEMP and DMPO were chosen as radical trapping agents, and EPR was used to investigate the ROS produced by the CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs + Cr(VI) reaction system. The EPR characteristic peaks corresponded to •OH, •O2 −, and 1 O2, respectively (Fig. S4A, B, and C). This result implies that Cr(VI) enhances CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs oxidase-like catalytic ability, enabling the oxidation of TMB. Moreover, to better understand the oxidase-like activity of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs and to obtain its kinetic parameters, we investigated the catalytic activity of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs under the optimal conditions by enzyme kinetic theory using TMB as the substrate. To conduct dynamic experiments, one substrate concentration was fixed while the other concentration was regularly and alternatively altered. CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs' Michaelis-Menten curves were evaluated for a variety of TMB concentrations (Fig. 4B), and the Michaelis constant (Km) and maximal response rate (Vmax) were obtained using the Lineweaver-Burk equation (Eq. (1)) (Fig. 4C).
(1)
1
v
=
K
m
V
max ⁡
[ S ]
+
1
V
max ⁡

Table S1 lists the Km and Vmax values of cerium-based nanomaterials such as CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs. Km is a crucial indicator of the enzyme substrate affinity. The Km value decreases when the enzyme affinity for the substrate increases and vice versa. The findings demonstrated that CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs with TMB as substrate had an apparent Km of 0.17 mM, which was less than that of previous ceria-based nanozymes. The above results indicate that CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs have better oxidase-like activities as well as a higher affinity for substrates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting the enzyme-mimic activity of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH value of the reaction solution, the concentration of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs, the concentration of TMB, and the reaction duration are the important elements influencing the optimal sensing characteristics of the system. Therefore, the above experimental conditions were carefully optimized. A0 and A denote the absorption signals in the presence and absence of Cr(VI), respectively (A = A0 - A). The effect of the buffer solution pH was first investigated, and the results showed that the absorbance at 652 nm increased and subsequently fell as the pH increased, reaching a maximum at pH 3.2 ( Fig. 5A). As a result, pH 3.2 was chosen as the best setting for detecting Cr(VI) in further studies. The concentration of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs as a catalyst has a significant impact on the system. As shown in Fig. 5B, the absorption signal increases with concentration in a specific range, but the detection sensitivity stabilizes as the concentration exceeds 2.50 mg·mL−1. Considering the cost of the experiment, 2.50 mg·mL−1 was chosen as the concentration of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs for the subsequent experiments. As shown in Fig. 5C, the ΔA value first increased as the TMB concentration grew, and then when the TMB concentration was above 0.35 mM, the signal value appeared to decline significantly as the TMB concentration climbed. Based on the experimental data, 0.35 mM of TMB is the ideal concentration in this system for further investigations. Following the above optimization procedure, the experimental time was finally optimized. When the reaction time was increased, the absorbance at 652 nm rose considerably between 2 and 6 minutes, according to the reaction time profile. After that, the absorbance value did not significantly alter. 6 min was chosen as the system reaction time based on the effectiveness of the assay and the ease of operation (Fig. 5D). In summary, the pH, CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs concentration, TMB concentration, and reaction duration for this experiment were 3.2, 2.50 mg·mL−1, 0.35 mM and 6 min, respectively. In addition, compared with previous methods based on nano-enzymatic activity for the detection of Cr(Ⅵ) ( Table 1), our proposed method is simple and inexpensive, and the increase in specific surface area affects the oxidase activity of the material, which results in a high sensitivity for the detection of Cr(Ⅵ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the capability of the CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs sensing platform for Cr(VI) detection, the linear response ranges and LOD were obtained under the previously indicated optimal experimental conditions. The oxidase-like activity of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs increased in response to a rise in Cr(VI) concentration, as Fig. 6 A illustrates. This led to an increase in the absorption peak at 650 nm and the creation of TMBox in various blue hues (the blue tint of the TMB solution rapidly deepened as the Cr(VI) content increased). Fig. 6B shows that the variation of absorbance with Cr(VI) concentration (ΔA). The concentration of Cr(VI) and the absorption signal at 652 nm showed an excellent linear connection in the range of 50 ∼ 5000 nM, with a fitting equation of ΔA = 0.1889 CCr(VI) + 0.0282 (R2 = 0.995). The calibration curve slope (S) and the test standard deviation (σ) determine the LOD, which was 42 nM. This method has a higher LOD than other large instruments, but the preparation of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs is relatively simple, inexpensive, and convenient. The WHO Drinking Water Quality Guidelines permit a maximum of 0.96 μM of total chromium in water, which is both within the detection range and well over our limit of detection. This implies that the technique we used to detect Cr(VI) in the aqueous environment is suitable.

The colorimetric sensor platform stability was then confirmed (Fig. S5). The findings indicate that the absorbance value changes only a little after 30 days of repeated reaction, and the sensor has good stability. Given the possibility of other interfering compounds in complex media like sewage and wastewater, the response of Cr(VI) and other ions to analytical techniques based on CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs is contrasted and examined. As Fig. 7 illustrates, when these interfering compounds are present, there is minimal reaction to coexisting substances and no discernible color change by naked-eye colorimetry. It is noteworthy that trace amounts of Cr(VI) contributed to a faster and more pronounced color response in the oxidation of TMB during the CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs-catalyzed oxidation process. The concentration of Cr(VI) can be indirectly measured by measuring the color change. In summary, it is anticipated that the colorimetric approach based on CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs will detect Cr(Ⅵ) in complicated samples such as sewage and wastewater in a highly selective manner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis application in Yellow River and tap water
ce:section.ce:para:
Cr(VI) was measured in tap water and Yellow River water, respectively, to confirm that the suggested approach could be applied to actual samples. A 0.22 μm membrane was used to filter actual water samples to remove silt. The recoveries of spiked Cr(VI) at various concentrations were tested three times (0.5, 1, and 2 μM) in tap water and Yellow River water. As shown in
Tables 2 and 3, the recoveries were comparable to those determined by ICP-MS, indicating that this system has good acceptability and accuracy for the analysis of Cr(VI) in environmental water samples. Compared with other colorimetric methods, this method has the advantages of high specificity, accuracy, reliability, economy and environmental protection, and the LOD is as low as 42 nM (LOD = 3σ/S, slope of calibration curve (S), standard deviation of test (σ)), which is suitable for the detection of low concentration of Cr (VI) in environmental water samples. The CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs used in this system have the ability to specifically recognize Cr(VI), which can eliminate more interference substances and reduce false alarms. The detection process does not require the use of hazardous reagents and does not generate hazardous waste, which is in line with the principles of green chemistry. In addition, the measurements of the method were validated by an agency-certified test method, which ensured accuracy while controlling the cost of the experiment. In summary, the detection of trace Cr(VI) in the aqueous environment based on enzyme-like activity has great potential, and the study of new materials or the development of novel nano-enzymes is a promising approach. To facilitate on-site detection, further research is being conducted to incorporate CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs into paper-based devices to develop a more practical sensing system.

In this work, N-CQDs were introduced for the first time to efficiently complex two metal oxides, and CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs in spherical morphology were synthesized, which showed high catalytic oxidation activity for TMB. In addition, trace Cr(VI) significantly accelerated the oxidation of TMB during the catalytic oxidation of CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs. Under ideal conditions, the colorimetric sensing system based on CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs showed a strong linear relationship with Cr(VI) concentration in the range of 50–5000 nM, with a detection limit of 42 nM, and the results of actual water samples verified the applicability of the CeO2/N-CQDs@CuO NPs, which revealed the potential of the system for the detection of Cr(VI) in the aqueous environment. potential of this system for the detection of Cr(VI) in aqueous environments. The discovery of this sensor is expected to create a unique portable high-throughput visualization platform for the detection of Cr(VI), which opens up a new approach for field testing of real water samples.

YuJin Li: Visualization, Validation. Mingzhu Qiu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. PeiQing Guo: Validation, Supervision, Investigation. XueFang Lei: Visualization, Project administration. xibin zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. ShaoHui Li: Visualization. Ran Meng: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21165016, and 21265018); the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province (No.s 1011GKCA025, and 1208RJZM289); the Postgraduate Research Funding Projects of Northwest Normal University (2022KYZZ-S187); and Outstanding Graduate Student Innovation Star project of Gansu (2023CXZX-361).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2024.112334.
